narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and middle-income family background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 40.5% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to help ensure students are set up for success. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic deciles from Years 1 and 3, where you ranked in the 2nd decile—much lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position—making these your biggest hurdles as they strongly pushed the model toward expecting bar exam struggles. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 37.50, above the average of 36.70, was your strongest asset, significantly pulling the prediction in favor of you passing the exam. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, though below the typical 3.21, still offered some positive influence toward success, as did your plan to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of students who do so. Your family income in the middle bracket also nudged things slightly toward passing, compared to varied incomes across applicants. Overall, while your LSAT and other factors showed promise, the lower early academic rankings outweighed them for this model, setting you apart from peers with stronger school deciles who typically see approvals. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes bar passage signals. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT prep to build on your strength, and reapplying with updated transcripts. You might also explore bridge programs or community college pathways that have helped students like you excel. We're rooting for you—law needs your voice, and with focused improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing; your story is just beginning.
